title: 🧠 The Dark Side of Meditation Rarely Discussed
description: When mindfulness goes wrong. Willoughby Britton's research, meditation-induced psychosis, and protocols that protect practitioners.

segments.voice: A 32-year-old yoga teacher returned from a ten-day silent retreat unable to sleep, hearing voices, and convinced reality was dissolving. She had meditated daily for years. The retreat broke something. Six months later, with antipsychotic medication and supportive therapy, she stabilized. Her case is one of hundreds documented by Brown University's Willoughby Britton in the Varieties of Contemplative Experience study. The benefits of meditation are real. The risks are also real and rarely mentioned.

Willoughby Britton runs the Clinical and Affective Neuroscience Laboratory at Brown University. She started studying difficult meditation experiences after her own retreat in the early 2000s produced unexpected effects. Patients began reaching out to her, having heard of a researcher who took these experiences seriously. By 2017, her landmark paper in PLOS ONE documented 60 case reports across multiple traditions. The pattern was consistent enough to warrant a clinical category.

The cases included anxiety, depression, dissociation, hyperarousal, perceptual changes, and in severe cases psychotic-like episodes. Some practitioners experienced months or years of disruption following intensive practice. The phenomenon has names across traditions. The dark night in Christian mysticism. The fall in Tibetan Buddhism. Kundalini syndrome in some yoga lineages. The traditions long acknowledged these states. Modern Western mindfulness instruction often did not.

Here's the catch. Most meditation research focuses on benefits in cooperative populations. Eight-week mindfulness-based stress reduction trials show modest improvements in anxiety, depression, and stress markers. Effect sizes around 0.4 to 0.6, meaningful but not transformative. Britton noted that adverse effects are rarely tracked even when they occur. A 2017 review by Nicholas van Dam at the University of Melbourne found that only 20 percent of meditation trials reported on adverse events. The rest didn't ask.

When researchers ask, the numbers shift. A 2022 study from Britton's lab surveyed 953 meditators with at least three years of practice. Twenty-five percent reported significant adverse effects lasting longer than a month. Twelve percent reported effects severe enough to interfere with daily functioning. The numbers were higher for those who had attended intensive retreats. Daily practice alone was lower risk. The dose-response relationship matched what intensive meditators had been reporting informally for years.

The mechanisms are starting to be mapped. Judson Brewer at Brown University, working alongside Britton, has used fMRI to track default mode network changes with meditation. The default mode network handles self-referential thought. Meditation reduces its activity. In moderate doses, this reduces rumination and improves wellbeing. In intense doses, the same reduction can produce derealization, the sense that the world isn't real. The same mechanism produces benefit at one dose and pathology at another.

Britton's clinical observation is that the issue isn't meditation itself but inadequate framing and support. Practitioners pushing through difficult states without guidance often worsen their condition. The traditional contemplative cultures had teachers who recognized these states and provided context. Modern apps and short workshops don't. The instruction often emphasizes more practice when distress arises. For some practitioners in some states, this advice deepens the problem rather than resolving it.

Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are sometimes used to manage meditation-induced symptoms. The pattern is unusual. Patients who started SSRI treatment after meditation often improved within weeks. The implication is that meditation can disturb serotonergic systems in ways pharmacology can repair. The mechanism may involve dorsal raphe nucleus changes during deep concentrative practice. The neuroscience is incomplete. The clinical observation is consistent across multiple practitioners reporting independently.

I tried a ten-day Vipassana retreat in 2019. The first three days felt grounding. Days four through six produced unexpected emotional release I wasn't prepared for. By day eight, sleep had become difficult and a low-grade dissociation set in. I completed the retreat and recovered within a few weeks once back in normal life. The experience was educational. The intensity wasn't advertised in the materials. Many participants seemed surprised by what came up. Few left. Most pushed through.

Catherine Kerr at Brown University, before her death in 2016, did imaging work on meditators showing both improvement and difficulty. Her data suggested some practitioners showed reduced amygdala connectivity in ways that improved emotional regulation. Others showed prefrontal-limbic changes that destabilized regulation. The same protocol produced opposite outcomes depending on the individual. Predicting in advance who would benefit and who would struggle proved difficult. The screening tools don't yet exist.

Trauma history is a key risk factor. Meditation can surface previously suppressed material in ways that overwhelm coping resources. David Treleaven, a clinical psychologist who wrote Trauma-Sensitive Mindfulness in 2018, has documented this pattern. Trauma survivors entering meditation programs without trauma-informed adaptation can deteriorate. The meditation isn't doing anything wrong. The standard instruction isn't designed for trauma physiology. Modifications are possible and effective. Most popular programs don't use them.

Treleaven's adaptations include eyes-open practice, anchoring to external rather than internal sensations, shorter durations, and explicit permission to stop. These modifications make meditation accessible to trauma survivors who would otherwise be harmed. The trauma-sensitive approach is now being adopted by some major training programs. The shift is slow. Most teacher training still doesn't include trauma considerations. Practitioners are often pushed into states their nervous systems aren't ready to handle.

But the brain has a workaround through dose adjustment. Daily fifteen-minute practice produces most measured benefits with minimal risk profile. Multi-day retreats deliver greater effects but at greater risk. The optimal dose is shorter than many programs prescribe. Britton's recommendation for retreats is daily check-ins with experienced teachers and screening for vulnerability factors before participation. These protocols exist but aren't universally enforced. Many retreat centers operate with informal supervision.

Depersonalization disorder can develop in vulnerable practitioners. Daphne Simeon at Mount Sinai studies this clinically. Subjects describe feeling that the world isn't real, that they're observing themselves from outside, that emotions are absent. The condition is debilitating when persistent. A subset of cases trace to intensive meditation. The treatment is challenging. Recovery often takes months to years. The risk is rare but real, and most meditators are unaware it exists.

Bhante Anālayo, a Buddhist scholar-monk at Hamburg University, has written about traditional safeguards in Theravada Buddhist practice. The classical curriculum includes psychoethical preparation before intensive concentration practice. Modern secular adaptations stripped these elements as religious. The stripping may have removed protective scaffolding. The traditional teachers selected practitioners carefully. The retreat structure included gradual progression. Modern access is more democratic and less protective.

Cheetah House, founded by Britton, provides support for meditators in distress. The organization receives calls weekly from practitioners in difficulty. The volume is enough to suggest the problem is more common than published research yet captures. Most callers had no idea adverse effects were possible. They had been told meditation only helps. The mismatch between expectation and experience compounds the distress. Education before practice would prevent some cases.

Insurance complications follow. Some practitioners hospitalized after meditation-induced episodes have struggled to get insurance coverage because the precipitating activity was considered voluntary. The legal categorization of these cases is unclear. Mental health professionals often don't have training in recognizing meditation-related states. A patient describing meditation-induced symptoms may be misdiagnosed. The diagnostic categories don't cleanly capture the phenomenology, which complicates appropriate treatment.

Mindfulness-based programs in schools have shown mixed results. A major UK trial published in 2022 randomized over 8,000 adolescents to either standard education or mindfulness curricula. The mindfulness group showed no significant improvement in mental health outcomes overall. A subgroup with pre-existing mental health concerns showed worsening on some measures. The findings forced reconsideration of school-based mindfulness as universally beneficial. Targeting matters.

Tim Lomas at Harvard's School of Public Health has written about the mindfulness conformity assumption. Programs assume practice produces uniformly beneficial effects. The assumption isn't supported by data. Practice produces benefits for some, neutral effects for others, and harm for a subset. The distribution is rarely communicated to participants. The marketing of mindfulness has been one-sided in ways that obscure the actual risk-benefit profile. Honest framing would help potential practitioners make informed choices.

Concentration practices appear higher risk than open monitoring practices. Concentration involves sustained focus on a single object. Breath. A mantra. A visual point. Open monitoring involves spacious awareness of whatever arises. The two practices produce different neural patterns. Concentration practice can drive states of unity, dissolution, and ego loss that some practitioners interpret positively and others find terrifying. The same experience can produce mystical insight or psychotic-like crisis depending on framing and stability.

Joseph Loizzo at the Nalanda Institute trained as both a psychiatrist and a Tibetan Buddhist scholar. His clinical work bridges contemplative and psychiatric perspectives. He distinguishes states that require integration from states that require medical management. The boundary is sometimes unclear. A practitioner experiencing what their tradition calls awakening may also be experiencing what psychiatry calls a psychotic break. The interpretation matters for outcome. Wrong interpretation in either direction can harm the person.

Selen Atasoy at Oxford has used novel mathematical approaches to map brain harmonics during altered states. Her work suggests meditation produces some of the same brain dynamics as psychedelics, in modulated form. Both can shift the brain toward higher entropy states. Higher entropy correlates with creative insight and ego dissolution. It also correlates with psychiatric symptoms in vulnerable individuals. The shared mechanism explains both the benefits and the risks across these distinct practices.

Recovery protocols for meditation casualties are being developed. The TraumaSensitiveMindfulness collaborative has published guidelines. Reduce or pause practice. Increase grounding through physical activity, social connection, and routine. Consult with practitioners experienced in adverse meditation effects. In severe cases, psychiatric medication may be appropriate. Recovery often takes months. Most practitioners eventually integrate the experience and return to modified practice. A minority experience persistent disruption requiring ongoing management.

What does the data say practitioners should do? Practice in moderation. Avoid intensive retreats without prior gradual buildup. Choose programs with experienced teachers and trauma-aware curricula. Screen for vulnerability factors honestly. Watch for warning signs: insomnia, persistent dissociation, intrusive thoughts, mood instability. Stop or modify practice if these arise rather than pushing through. The traditional advice to deepen practice during difficulty is wrong for many modern practitioners without traditional support structures.

The deepest finding is that meditation is a powerful intervention with real effects in both directions. The framing of mindfulness as a uniformly safe wellness practice has done a disservice to practitioners who encountered the harder territory. Honest discussion of risks alongside benefits would protect more people. The benefits are real for most practitioners. The risks are real for a meaningful minority. Both deserve attention. The contemplative traditions knew this. Modern dissemination has been slow to relearn it.

Subscribe to MindWired and hit the bell. Next week's episode goes deeper into neuroplasticity after 40, the latest research on how adult brains rebuild and what protocols actually work for cognitive maintenance.
